steps.title: Identify what Text 2 says about Blackwell's findings
steps.content:
Text 2 explains that many predation relief studies show increased prey abundance and often focus on small, rapidly reproducing prey (birds, mice, frogs). White-tailed deer — Potvin's study subject — are large and reproduce slowly, which could explain why they respond differently. The key factor is the physical characteristics of the prey species.
  • Choice A — ‘They may have been different if the ratio of the body size of stoats to that of house mice was greater than it is.’: Text 2 never addresses the relative body size of predators to prey as an explanatory factor. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘They may reflect a response that does not tend to be observed in prey species other than house mice.’: Contradicts Text 2, which says many studies show the same response in small, rapidly reproducing prey, not just mice. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘They may be partially explained by the fact that stoats are smaller and reproduce more rapidly than wolves.’: Text 2 never compares predator traits (stoats vs. wolves); the explanation is about prey characteristics, not predator characteristics. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘They may be partially attributable to physical characteristics of house mice that are not shared by white-tailed deer.’: Directly matches Text 2's explanation: small, fast-reproducing prey are more likely to show increased abundance; large, slow-reproducing prey such as white-tailed deer may not. ✓
That confirms D is correct.

steps.title: Confirm the reasoning
steps.content:
Text 2 offers prey characteristics as a possible explanation for the differing results: small, fast-reproducing prey are more likely to show the increased-abundance response; large, slow-reproducing prey such as white-tailed deer may not. The correct response restates this as “physical characteristics of house mice that are not shared by white-tailed deer,” which is precisely Text 2's logic.

That confirms D is correct.
